rationale: The cited paper is used for more than a passing literature list: the citing article explicitly draws on hedonic versus utilitarian shopping work (including Dhar and Wertenbroch 2000) to motivate its intrinsic-versus-extrinsic choice framing. It then uses that distinction in its own design, especially Study 4, where hedonic (want) versus utilitarian (need) framing operationalizes experiential versus instrumental choice mode. This is a meaningful conceptual and operational borrowing that helps structure hypotheses and interpretation, even though the citing paper studies different outcomes (mental resources and post-choice interest).
relation_description: The citing paper applies Dhar and Wertenbroch's hedonic-versus-utilitarian choice distinction to construct and justify a manipulation of choice mode (experiential vs instrumental). It transfers that distinction to a new dependent-variable domain, testing effects on depletion/energization and willingness to act on the chosen option.
